speaker: interviewer
text: Gisela Konopka. Tape number two.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said that the rabbi helped. I didn't know him, and I don't remember his name. But I think the major point is that he discussed my questions with me. And that he-- again, I always repeat that-- he showed me respect. This has all influenced my whole teaching, my whole life, is that when you show respect to another person, they listen. It doesn't mean you convince them. But at least, we can communicate and talk about it. And he then told my father he shouldn't shut me in. I would be all right. And that helped.

speaker: interviewer
text: Shut you in--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my father said that he would close me-- I mean, I wasn't allowed to go out at all and meet my friends. And the youth movement was my life. That was my lifeblood and my life. I think it's, it's not that-- I have to say, it's not that I rejected Judaism. That is the wrong way to say it. But it's not the idea that I believe everything that is said in the Bible, nor that I have to belong to one, only, denomination. I still have-- I read a lot of-- later, much later, in-- about Buddhism, about other-- and I'm not a Buddhist whatsoever. But when I was in India, it interested me, what the different religions are. And it doesn't seem to me that we have to all have these separations. I still do that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you continue to be involved, then, with Jewish activities or with the Jewish--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I was involved. I continued with--

speaker: interviewer
text: --youth movement?

speaker: interviewee
text: --That youth movement. It was very, very strong. We belonged, very much, together. Now, I have to say, I also had my own group. That's not a different group. That's just subgroups of that same organization. And my little sister was in that. And some other people were in that. One I want to mention because later, she was killed by the Nazis in the worst ways, Hilda Monte, who has written quite a lot. M-O-N-T-E. Or she's also known as Hilda Meisel, M-E-I-S-E-L.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened was that slowly, we became, all, very interested in politics. You couldn't be in the 1929-- is it '29? Yes. In that period-- '28, '29-- without not getting interested in politics.

speaker: interviewee
text: Rathenau-- R-A-T-H, I think, E-N-A-U-- was a Jewish prominent-- I forgot what he was-- politician. And he was murdered. And in our school, no teacher wanted-- so the German republic said, there has to be an honoring of that person.

speaker: interviewer
text: No teacher wanted to talk about it, because they were all very right wing in my school. Did I say that I had to cry myself into school? Oh, dear. I forgot that. Can I interrupt for a minute? Please, go ahead--

speaker: interviewer
text: --and talk about school.

speaker: interviewee
text: --certainly went, as a child at age 6, into what's called lyceum, L-Y-C-E-U-M. But after three years, you could transfer to a gimnasium, G-I-M-N-A-S-I-U-M. And-- but my parents had no money. And they had already one daughter, Hanna, who wanted to go and was allowed to go to the gimnasium. And you had to pay for that. And I wanted it very badly. And I remember, again, I'm a crying child. I cried and cried. And my mother said, any child can make a living sewing. And I said, I can never make a living sewing. I probably couldn't anyhow. But there I was. And so I-- finally, my father said, when that child wants to learn that much, let her learn. And so I got into the gimnasium. So I have to say that, that that's an addition to that. And school was very exciting, very stimulating. At the same time, it's very upsetting. Most of the teachers, as I say, were very right wing, during-- early, during that time. Once, there was a swastika already at the blackboard. This is early. This is '27 or something like that, or even earlier. I can't remember the dates anymore. And I objected, and some others objected. And the teacher was mad at me. And when I came home, my father said, Jews are supposed to suffer and not to fight. And naturally, it made me very angry because he had been a socialist and a fighter too. So, but I'm saying there were all these different points.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I'm skipping an awful lot. And can I mention that I wrote a book much later? I called it And there is much more in it. But I can't say all of these things.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyhow, there comes this politicizing of all of this movement. And many of my very close friends went to the young communists because that were-- and they are Jews. They were the most-- they thought, most idealistic, most active, fighting for justice.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then we are about 17. We are still in school. And I, myself, and a few others didn't want to go there. And when I think about it, it's rather strange because I felt just as strongly as they about justice and so on.

speaker: interviewer
text: But I just didn't like the communists because they were very [? odd-- ?] how do you call that? Very-- well, everything had to be learned exactly the way they said it. You know? There's a word for it that I can't think right now.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was-- I didn't like that. And I also didn't like, especially, the enormous violence that they were preaching. But that does not mean that I always think all of those who went to become communists were bad people. They were very idealistic and very hopeful. And under the Nazis, many of them were killed. I mentioned [? Hans ?] [? Litton. ?] I want to mention Rudi Arndt, who was one of the best. The East Germans once published a-- there is a stamp, a postage stamp, with his name on it. And Rudi, I'm told, was a saint, if you want to call it, in the concentration camp. He gave everybody strength. He was certainly killed too. And there are others. So-- and I have to say, several that I know were also-- went to Russia and then finally were killed in Russia by the Stalinists. So there we are. The world is crazy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Going back to 1927--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You first remember seeing a swastika in school.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. In the school, at one point. There were swastikas, at times, in the streets, at the walls. You know, the right wingers were doing that already. And I remember having little pocketknives, always, and we would scratch off of the walls. I mean, we'd just-- it's not-- I always say, anti-Semitism is so closely related to anything that I call very right wing. And well, I'm worried about it because it always means-- they make beautiful phrases about home, and women should be nice and all of that kind of stuff. But at the same time, it's always against some race, against somebody who is considered inferior. But this is already in 1927. And I'm going very quick now. I have forgotten the exact year-- I think it's 1929-- that I'm making what is called the abitur, A-B-I-T-U-R. It's the graduation from the gimnasium. And then I do something that must have hurt my parents very much. Today, I understand that they must have been very hurt, because I insisted that, I cannot go to the university right away-- here, I was crying for that-- but that I have to become a laborer in a factory. I have to know how it feels to make your living that way. Again, not unusual for some of those idealistic young people. But I do understand that it was hard for them. I also felt I had to leave Berlin, that I had to go somewhere else, just start differently. And we're doing this as fast as I can.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to Hamburg. Oh, I forgot that when this politicizing came, I had to find a group because we had split. And I find-- found another group. It was the ISK, I-S-K.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which stood for--

speaker: interviewee
text: That stands for Internationale Sozialistische Kampfbund. K-A-M-P-F-B-U-N-D. And they are-- they were, for a long time, part of the Social Democratic Party. They separated from it because they were more active than they felt the Social Democrats were. They felt they were becoming too soft. And they had a leader, Leonard Nelson, N-E-L-S-O-N, who was a university professor at Goettingen, G-O-E-T-T-I-N-G-E-N. And he had started a special school, because his idea was that democracy doesn't work. You have to educate people to be totally selfless. Oh, there's a lot there that I don't agree with it anymore. But at the time, it was something. I met these people. Among them, I'll give a name, Maria Hoddann, H-O-D-D-A-N-N. And she was a very intelligent woman, very thoughtful. And I felt that this was maybe-- I didn't feel as close to them as to the old youth movement, but I would be part of it, and I would have something-- what I liked there, especially, was the great mixture, and really, a mixture, of intellectuals and working people.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you would talk together? What kinds of things would you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we would. There were all these evenings where one would discuss things. There was learning a lot about working class problems. And this particular group, for instance, it was not for collectivization of the farmland, but they were for cutting down the big farm-- the landowners.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they belonged to a group. There's a famous professor, Oppenheim, O-P-P-E-N-H-E-I-M, who had the theory that one can work with small farmers. I'm just giving examples. We learned a lot about economics. We learned a lot about thinking, Socratic method-- how do you really think about problems?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, there was a lot of-- I-- and strange things, perhaps, today, but for us, some very important. I grew up in the west of Berlin. I had been, a lot, in the east because many of my relatives were very poor and lived in the eastern part of Berlin. And also, I have to say-- and I say things at the side-- that I never understood the separation of Western and Eastern Jews. We were one. I mean-- [LAUGHS] But I had grown up in the western part of Berlin. And they made us-- they said we should sit for a day and a night in one of those awful movies on the, on the part in the slums of Berlin. And sit there and see what goes on. And it was very important, because we saw, suddenly, that these were all people, sitting in those movies, who had no place to live. They didn't see the movie. They were sleeping. To see that-- I mean, I could go into a lot about all that. It's just an awful lot. And-- [COUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So I belonged to the ISK. I decided to go to Hamburg because there was a very good ISK group. So I left with-- I think I still remember it was 90 mark. I had always earned my own living, my clothing, and so on.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I will try to do this fast. Otherwise, I will never get, I think, to the Nazi period. I lived in one little room where it-- I learned, a lot, how people live, the yelling, the beating of women, the beating of children. You heard it all of the time. On the other hand, there were these people from the ISK who were wonderful and very helpful to me. And I was new. And then I went to the unemployment office. And this is high unemployment. It's 1929. I forgot that this was preceded by inflation, but I won't go into all of that. And I worked in a factory, where it was dirty, and filthy, and the women were exploited in the worst way.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, yeah. That's difficult to explain. I don't know whether anybody in this life still knows bottles that are closed with something with metal that closes over something. I don't know how that's called. You made those. You put them together very quick, hundreds, if possible, in a few minutes, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: By hand.

speaker: interviewee
text: By hand. And you sit on long tables that are very dirty and filthy. And you do that. And I was always very imbued, and that has really been my life work for a long time, with, with labor unions. I always felt they were combining all of these different parties and were more reasonable and so on. None of those women were in a labor union. I certainly started trying to get them into the labor unions. The labor unions told me that they couldn't do anything because we weren't yet organized, which taught me, again, how some bureaucracies are [INAUDIBLE] everywhere. But the owners of that place found out that I was a union member and another woman was. And we were thrown out. So we lost the job in the beautiful German Republic. And after that, I got into another factory. That was-- is still, to me, today, a very significant experience. And I wouldn't have missed it for anything. I-- it was-- again, I was sitting at a machine. I can only do this, make those movements, all of the time, with some-- I can't describe it. With some steel that had to be hammered this way, that way, all of the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the end product?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pieces of steel that were put into forms in which, later, cement was poured. I can't tell you how it's called. I can't even say it in German or in English. The point is that I never forget what it does to do this for hours on end, and how that numbs your mind. And I had to, again, I know poems still today, hundreds by heart. I had to say those poems. I had to sing, and I have a horrible voice. But this is what you had to do. In addition to that, there were people in the labor unions. So they were-- that was good. I also saw how people had to live. There were women who had small children. And they had to bring them, first, to someplace where they took care of them. And then we talked about that. And I became good friends with some of these women. And one of them, I helped to go to a doctor, who was a wonderful doctor in the slums of Hamburg, who helped me to learn that as a doctor, you don't only ask what hurts on your body-- you ask what this person has to suffer in other ways. That was very important. I learned anti-Semitism is everywhere. There was one woman who thought, first, she was my friend. But then she saw my hands, and she said, hey, you are not one of us because your hands are too clean. Because in that work, you get very hands embedded with some dirt and stuff like that. And I said, well, I was at home. You know, I didn't want to say what-- where I really came from. And then she said, and you are a Jew. Jews don't work in factories. That's another one. And then the other one, next, would say, leave her alone. She's as good as anybody else. She works just as hard. This is the kind of thing I learned. I learned an awful lot, at that time, that I could go for hours to talk about. The-- I would have-- I stayed-- I mean, I worked, I think, a year in a factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: As a Jew, then, you were separate. You were separated, in terms of how people felt towards you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not some-- not separated. I was part of the union movement. No. I only say that, one-- I could hear somebody making dumb remarks like that. But then I would say, so what? Deny-- I have never denied I'm Jewish. You know, that doesn't mean I always have to-- the religious identification. But I will never deny. There was a film, much later, that I saw in the United States, and the man says, I'm a Jew by discrimination. And I think that's a very good way of putting it. But I will not go into all of the details. It was a very important learning experience. Mona Specht, S-P-E-C-H-T, who later became very famous after the war working in the Republic, helping with the United Nations for children and so on. Mina Specht said to me, you'd better go and study again. You are capable of doing this. This is-- when somebody from the 'movement' said it, I listened a little more. And so I started at the University of Hamburg, studying psychology, history, philosophy, and especially education. I wanted to go into elementary school because that's where the poor children were. I have to say that the most exciting thing at the University, for me, was, first, that it was clean. I have-- and all my life, I say, rich people don't understand what it is to live in dirt all of the time. And this was really exciting. It was clean, but it was also eating what you could get. And studies at Hamburg were exciting, were superb. Psychology, especially because that was William Stern, who was known, also, in the United States. He was-- by the way, William Stern certainly was also Jewish. But he was one of the psychology professors who had such an enormously wide view of psychology. He didn't have to say, Freud is wrong. Oh, I forgot to tell, certainly, that we all grew up with all of this. But he also gave us pros and cons on several sides. He was especially interested in child psychology. I learned a lot about how to observe children. I'm not going into all of that. And I will say something, whether or not he's come to power.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this is the period of three years at the University. At the same time, I'm still active in the labor movement, in the ISK. Meanwhile, I get to know the person who later was my husband-- much, much later. But we went on trips together-- bicycling, riding-- with a lot of groups. Always fighting because he said, people are bad and we have to help them to become good. What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. Well, it's Erhardt-- E-R-H-A-R-D-T-- Paul-- P-A-U-L-- Konopka-- K-O-N-O-P-K-A. But we-- I still fight, on the bicycle, about he said, people are bad and we have to help them to become better. And I said, people are born good and we have made them bad. So this was the great discussions we had. Also, I sometimes was so annoyed because he was so capable and he knew, always, everything, what to do. And I was always clumsy with my hands. But this was-- and at that time, my younger sister came, once, to visit, Ruth, to whom I was very, very close.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I didn't go into all of that. Ruth and I were very close. We were sisters under the skin. And I'll never forget that one evening, she says, you know, the one for you is that one. And she pointed to Paul. And I said, oh, no. Not at all. And she says, you'll see. It's very interesting that she saw it so much earlier. Were you working at that time, as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. No. I was studying at the University, but I certainly had no money. So I always gave not a-- oh, how's it called? How do you call it when you help somebody through school? You teach them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tutoring?

speaker: interviewee
text: Tutoring. Thank you for the word. I was tutoring all of the time. But I had very interesting experiences tutoring. Again, I can't talk about all of that.

speaker: interviewer
text: But for instance, there was a young boy who was blind. And I was tutoring him. And I've never forgotten how that kid would help me carry my bicycle up two flights. And I said, you-- how can you do that? He says, I can feel it, can't I? And his father told me, when-- one day, when the father said something, how terrible that he was blind, he said, Father, what can you see? And the father described. He said, can you see the street? No. So I don't miss very much, do I?

speaker: interviewee
text: But this boy was a genius. That happens to be also a Jewish boy, and he was killed under the Nazis as not only being Jewish, but also useless because he was blind.

speaker: interviewer
text: I will name one other, because they stand out in my mind. And he was Chinese. He came from China. His sister belonged, also, to the ISK. And they were very interested in these movements. And he-- and it was almost ridiculous when he had to learn, in school, all of these German classics. You know?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: But I never forgot when I once saw him, when he came, and his lip was split, and his face was discolored. And I say, what has happened? And he says, well, the kids made, always, fun of me. And they said I should jump. And they showed me the shallow part in the pool and made me jump in the shallow part. And so he got hurt.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said, I'm going with you to that school and we object. And he said, no, we don't. I handle that myself. You don't. There, again, the pride of the young. I learned a lot from that. Now, I could give a lot. But no, I always earned my living. I lived in very, very poor situation. And OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like to graduate from--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Now--

speaker: interviewer
text: --the University?

speaker: interviewee
text: --That's-- but now comes the Nazi period. And I have to start it on that. First of all, I have to say, now, that Paul and I became closer and closer. And we were-- lots of hiking together. And there's a beginning of-- and I have to say that the Nazis-- this is 1932, something like that. And I am getting very angry. And I have to say that right now. When people always talk about the Nazis as if it started with the war, which is 1939 and 1940. They did not. They started 1933. And the cruelty and the killing, all this started in '33. There wasn't, yet, the extermination camps. But the camps were later. And I'll go into that in a minute. All over. But I have to say it at this moment. And if somebody ever says that they don't know what the Nazis stood for, they could have known. Hitler published. You know, he was in prison for awhile, so he published this that book, K-A-M-P-F. And it said everything. But we begin to feel that we had to fight Nazis. There were already brown shirts, and I think, very often, people who were unemployed because they got three marks a week. And that was wonderful. They'd bought them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And there-- that was already-- I'm talking before they come to power. OK? But it was all there already, all of this. They got the money-- they got three marks-- the money from the Nazi party?

speaker: interviewee
text: From the party for one week. And they were-- well, I mean, I always take it as a picture, always, like there is a big demonstration of the Nazis, in the streets of the Republic-- it's still the Republic-- with spades over their shoulders because they were not allowed to wear any other arms. And Paul and I go down to see what's going on. And suddenly, the spades just fly on people who stand by, so that they get bloody. They were beating people. They were constantly beating.

speaker: interviewer
text: I stood next to a policeman who was not a Nazi. And he said, God, help us when those people get into power. And yeah? So I think I can start with the Nazi period. And then I'll talk about examinations.
